perturbation.original_text: FASC shall give prompt notice to the Adviser of the assertion of any claim or liability which is reasonably likely to result in a claim for indemnification under this Section; provided that the failure to give such notice, or any delay in giving such notice, shall not lessen the obligation of the Adviser to indemnify FASC except to the extent it results in actual prejudice.
perturbation.changed_text: FASC shall give notice to the Adviser of the assertion of any claim or liability which is reasonably likely to result in a claim for indemnification under this Section within 180 days; provided that the failure to give such notice, or any delay in giving such notice, shall not lessen the obligation of the Adviser to indemnify FASC, regardless of prejudice.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text sets a fixed 180-day deadline for notice and eliminates prejudice as a factor in indemnification, potentially conflicting with Pennsylvania law regarding timely notice and fairness in contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding reasonable notice and prejudice in contract claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires reasonable notification in contractual disputes to avoid prejudice. Setting a fixed 180-day limit and removing the prejudice requirement could be deemed unreasonable and contradict the principle of good faith and fair dealing implied in Pennsylvania contract law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a fixed 180-day deadline for providing notice of a claim and removes the requirement for actual prejudice to be demonstrated for the adviser to avoid indemnification. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation indicate this may contradict Pennsylvania law regarding timely notice and fairness in contractual obligations, specifically the principle of good faith and fair dealing implied in Pennsylvania contract law. The original contract stipulated that failure to give prompt notice would only lessen the obligation to indemnify 'to the extent it results in actual prejudice.' The revised contract eliminates this prejudice requirement, creating a contradiction where the adviser must indemnify regardless of whether they suffered any prejudice from the delayed notice. Since the prompt notification and prejudice consideration are removed, it creates an inconsistency with the original contract and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the remaining provisions of this Section, the term of this Agreement shall begin on the effective date first above written and shall continue until terminated by mutual agreement of the parties hereto or by either party on not less than 60 days' written notice to the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the remaining provisions of this Section, the term of this Agreement shall begin on the effective date first above written and shall continue until terminated by mutual agreement of the parties hereto or by either party on not less than 10 days' written notice to the other party hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days may be considered unreasonable and could conflict with fiduciary duties under the Investment Company Act of 1940, especially concerning registered investment companies. Pennsylvania law generally requires reasonable notice, and 10 days might be too short for complex financial arrangements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 indirectly requires reasonable notice in termination provisions by emphasizing the fiduciary responsibilities of investment advisors and the need for boards to act in the best interests of shareholders. A drastically shortened notice period (10 days) might hinder the board's ability to find suitable replacements or protect shareholder interests, potentially violating these fiduciary duties.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days. This contradicts Section 15(a)(3) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states that contracts must provide that they may be terminated 'on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser'. The modified text sets a deadline that violates federal law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto; provided, however, that no Amendment to this Agreement shall be effective with respect to any investment advisory services to be provided to any Account which is registered investment company unless, to the extent required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, such amendment has been approved both by the vote of a majority of the Board of the Account, including a majority of the members of the Board who are not parties to this Agreement or interested persons of any such party (other than as members of the Board), cast in person at a meeting called for that purpose and, where required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, on behalf of the Account by a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such Account as defined in Section 2(a)(42) of the 1940 Act.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto; provided, however, that no Amendment to this Agreement shall be effective with respect to any investment advisory services to be provided to any Account which is registered investment company unless such amendment has been approved by the Adviser.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the requirement for board and shareholder approval for amendments related to investment advisory services for registered investment companies, contravening Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 mandates that any material amendment to an investment advisory agreement with a registered investment company must be approved by both the board of directors (including a majority of independent directors) and, in some cases, by a vote of the shareholders. The modified text eliminates these mandatory approval processes, directly violating the statute's requirements for investor protection and oversight.

(b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for board and shareholder approval for amendments related to investment advisory services for registered investment companies, replacing it with approval only by the Adviser. This directly contradicts the mandate in Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which, as supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2 (15 U.S. Code § 80a-15), requires such contracts to be approved by a vote of the majority of the outstanding voting securities. The altered text undermines investor protection and oversight by eliminating required approval processes, establishing a clear legal inconsistency as it sets a requirement that violates federal law, which aligns directly with the definition for Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.
